one_line_takeaway: Deep-learning vulnerability detectors that report ~90-99% F1 on curated commit-level datasets collapse to near-zero precision and false-positive counts in the hundreds of thousands when evaluated on whole realistic codebases; the paper contributes the Real-Vul dataset to expose this gap.
key_finding: All four detectors show a catastrophic precision/F1 collapse in the realistic whole-codebase setting despite high accuracy inflated by class imbalance. On the Real-Vul test set (RQ1) precision drops by up to 86 pp (DeepWukong: 87%->1%) and 95 pp for LineVul (96%->1%), with F1 falling to 2-17%; AUC falls to 58-62%, near random. t-SNE shows heavy overlap between vulnerable and uncertain embeddings, explaining the false-positive flood. Training on Real-Vul (RQ2) does not fix it: imbalanced training collapses precision/recall/F1 to ~0% (models predict everything 'uncertain'); balanced training helps marginally (best F1 36% for ReVeal). Dead-code augmentation gives the biggest lift to the text-based LineVul (AUC +30.4 pp to 81.8%, F1 +26 pp to 46%) but little to graph-based models. Models detect information-leak/tainted-input CWEs (SFP clusters 895/896) best and path-traversal/predictability/miscellaneous (893/905/907) worst - no model detected ANY cluster-907 vulnerability - and they perform WORST on high-severity vulnerabilities. Fine-tuned 7B LLMs (CodeLlama AUC 67%, Mixtral 71%) beat DeepWukong/ReVeal/IVDetect but still trail the specialized LineVul.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is (a) Real-Vul, a new C/C++ vulnerability-detection dataset built to mirror realistic deployment (scanning entire codebases rather than only functions touched by a fixing commit) and to fix the label-inconsistency problem; and (b) an empirical re-evaluation showing that four state-of-the-art deep-learning vulnerability detectors (DeepWukong, LineVul, ReVeal, IVDetect) collapse when tested in this realistic setting. Real-Vul is derived from the top 10 projects by vulnerability count in the Big-Vul dataset (these 10 projects hold 73% of Big-Vul's vulnerable samples) and includes complete project snapshots, so vulnerable functions are rare relative to the vast majority of 'uncertain' (non-vulnerable-or-not-yet-reported) functions, matching real deployment. The authors also diagnose WHY models fail (embedding overlap, overfitting to tokens, project size, cyclomatic complexity) and propose a dead-code augmentation mitigation. NOTE: the SVM/CNN/LSTM/Devign/Vuldeepecker/Wild-C work in Section 8 is cited Related Work, NOT this paper's contribution; this paper only introduces Real-Vul and re-evaluates the four named models plus two LLMs.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-security paper; there is no attacker. This is a measurement/benchmarking study about the reliability of defensive ML tooling. The implicit concern is a false sense of security: prior evaluations report up to 99% recall/F1, but if such models were deployed to scan whole codebases they would emit enormous numbers of false positives (e.g., 114,629 to 512,284 FPs across the four models on Real-Vul's test set), making them impractical and potentially causing real vulnerabilities to be missed amid the noise. No exploit, poisoning, or evasion adversary is modeled or claimed.
limitations: Real-Vul is built from only 10 open-source C/C++ projects, limiting generalizability. All 'uncertain' functions are assumed non-vulnerable, but undiscovered vulnerabilities may lurk among them (a threat inherited from all prior datasets). Balanced datasets were created by a single random undersample; the authors acknowledge results could vary across draws but could not repeat runs due to limited compute. Residual label inconsistencies from whitespace/newline variations may persist despite regex normalization. The study covers only deep-learning detectors, not static/dynamic analysis tools. The overfitting/LIME analysis was restricted to LineVul (the Joern-based graph models could not be traced back to source), and its key positive-token hypothesis test was NOT statistically significant (p=0.13). Reported metric counts are somewhat inconsistent across sections (Section 3 reports 1,682,713 uncertain functions; the Implication section reports 270,919 total samples), which the paper does not reconcile.

datasets_used: Real-Vul (this paper's contribution; 10 projects from Big-Vul, complete codebase snapshots)
Big-Vul (source dataset; C/C++ functions from 348 GitHub projects, used to seed Real-Vul and train LineVul)
SARD (synthetic dataset used to train/replicate DeepWukong)
ReVeal dataset (Chromium + Debian Kernel, used to replicate ReVeal)
IVDetect dataset (FFmpeg/Qemu/Chromium, used to replicate IVDetect)
quantitative_results: Real-Vul composition: 5,528 vulnerable functions (4,418 train / 1,110 test) and 1,682,713 uncertain functions (769,464 train / 913,249 test); the 10 projects cover 73% of Big-Vul's vulnerable samples
Replication baselines on original datasets (Acc/Prec/Rec/F1/AUC): DeepWukong 98/87/98/93/88; LineVul 96/96/84/90/85; ReVeal 81/29/59/38/78; IVDetect 85/39/63/24/83
RQ1 on Real-Vul test set: DeepWukong 91/1/87/2/62; LineVul 89/1/90/2/58; ReVeal 89/10/80/17/61; IVDetect 85/2/84/2/59 (precision drops up to 95 pp, F1 up to 91 pp)
False positives on Real-Vul test: DeepWukong 439,494; LineVul 114,629; ReVeal 320,128; IVDetect 512,284
RQ2 imbalanced training: DeepWukong 99/0/0/0/50; LineVul 99/0/0/0/50; ReVeal 95/2/10/3/50.1; IVDetect 81/2/1/0/49.6
RQ2 balanced training: DeepWukong 89/1/53/2/51.4; LineVul 99/11/99/20/51.4; ReVeal 91/31/45/36/51.3; IVDetect 83/8/32/6/52.3
Dead-code augmentation (Table 6): DeepWukong 91/4/48/7/58; LineVul 98/37/65/46/81.8 (AUC +30.4 pp, F1 +26 pp vs balanced); ReVeal 92/33/47/38/57; IVDetect 78/10/35/16/53
Fine-tuned LLMs on Real-Vul (Table 8): CodeLlama-7B 80/34/54/42/67; Mixtral-7B 83/38/59/46/71 (AUC lags LineVul by 14.8 and 10.8 pp)
Per-severity F1 (low/medium/high): DeepWukong 18.26/20.56/9.7; LineVul 20.11/21.28/10.89; ReVeal 21.66/26.57/10.07; IVDetect 15.08/17.46/9.06 (severity mix: 60.5% medium, 33.9% low, 5% high)

Best per-SFP-cluster F1: DeepWukong 26.34%, LineVul 27.10%, ReVeal 33.61%, IVDetect 21.60%; best clusters 895 (info leak) / 896 (tainted input, code injection); no model detected any cluster-907 vulnerability
Label inconsistency: 15% of vulnerable samples in the ReVeal dataset were also listed as uncertain
Diagnostic stats: cyclomatic complexity Mann-Whitney U TP-vs-FP p=0.043, TN-vs-FN p=0.014; LIME positive-token ratio FP-vs-TP p=0.13 (not significant)
baselines_compared: DeepWukong (GNN over XFGs/PDG subgraphs, trained on SARD)
LineVul (CodeBERT sequence model over token chunks, trained on Big-Vul)
ReVeal (GNN over Code Property Graphs, uses SMOTE for balancing)
IVDetect (representation learning + graph-based interpretation over PDGs)
